facts.label: THE MIRACLE WORKER
facts.story: Serbian coach Bora Milutinović took charge of Costa Rica only months before the 1990 World Cup, the country's first-ever appearance in the competition, and promptly reshuffled the roster around him. Los Ticos beat Scotland and Sweden, lost narrowly to Brazil, and reached the round of 16 in their tournament debut — a run so improbable it helped earn Milutinović his nickname 'the miracle worker' and, later, the United States job. He remains the only coach to guide four different nations past a World Cup group stage.

facts.label: STRIPES FOR PARTIZAN
facts.story: Costa Rica's red shirt, blue shorts, and white socks trace back to the national flag, but their 1990 away kit told a different story: black-and-white vertical stripes borrowed from Club Sport La Libertad, one of the country's oldest clubs. Milutinović, a boyhood fan of Yugoslav side Partizan Belgrade, was said to be delighted the design also echoed his own team's colors. The striped shirt survived as Costa Rica's second choice until 1997, when a plainer white kit finally replaced it.

1990.headline: Milutinović's Miracle Debut

annotations.label: TEENAGE HEADER
annotations.story: Nineteen-year-old Ronald González rose to head in Costa Rica's only goal of the round of 16 mauling by Czechoslovakia, becoming the youngest goalscorer of the 1990 tournament. It was scant consolation as Tomáš Skuhravý's hat-trick ended the run, but it capped a stunning debut for a team given no chance of even qualifying.

annotations.label: CAYASSO SHOCKS SCOTLAND
annotations.story: Costa Rica opened their World Cup existence by beating Scotland — a five-time-consecutive qualifier — 1–0 in Genoa. Juan Cayasso struck first-time past goalkeeper Jim Leighton, a result so unthinkable that Scotland boss Andy Roxburgh turned visibly tetchy fielding questions about it afterward.

annotations.label: A PRESIDENT'S GIFT
annotations.story: Cayasso's winner over Scotland turned him into a national hero overnight; Costa Rica's president rewarded him with a brand-new Toyota Corolla. For a squad given no realistic chance of even qualifying, the win reframed an entire nation's relationship with the World Cup.

1990.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, white socks (Lotto)
1990.awayColors: black-and-white vertical stripes, white shorts — an homage to Club Sport La Libertad, worn in place of the home red for two group matches

2002.headline: So Close, Edged on Goals

annotations.label: TWO PAST THE CHAMPIONS
annotations.story: Costa Rica lost 5–2 to eventual champions Brazil, but Paulo Wanchope and Rónald Gómez both beat Marcos to become two of only a handful of players to score against Brazil's title-winning defense that summer — a rare crack in a team that conceded just four goals all tournament.

annotations.label: THE STRIPES ARE GONE
annotations.story: By 2002, the black-and-white striped away shirt that had defined Costa Rica since 1990 was long retired, replaced in 1997 by a plainer all-white change kit. Lotto's home shirt kept the flag's red and blue but dropped the Partizan-inspired flourish entirely.

annotations.label: GUIMARÃES'S FULL CIRCLE
annotations.story: Alexandre Guimarães came on as a substitute in the 1990 win over Scotland and again against Brazil under Bora Milutinović. Twelve years later he led Costa Rica back to the World Cup himself — this time from the touchline, as head coach for the 2002 campaign.

2002.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, white socks

2006.headline: Wanchope's Brace, Opening Rout

annotations.label: HIGHEST-SCORING OPENER
annotations.story: Paulo Wanchope's brace in Costa Rica's 4–2 defeat to hosts Germany produced the highest-scoring World Cup opening match since the tournament adopted a standalone opener in 1974, with Philipp Lahm and Miroslav Klose among the scorers for the hosts on the other end.

annotations.label: JOMA'S BRUSHSTROKE
annotations.story: Joma's 2006 shirt ran a horizontal band of white-and-blue brushstroke marks across the chest of an otherwise plain red jersey, with blue trim on the round collar and cuffs — a subtler design than the flag-referencing kits that came before or after it.

annotations.label: NO POINTS, NO GOALS CONCEDED SPARED
annotations.story: Sixteen years after their miracle debut run to the round of 16, Costa Rica left Germany 2006 without a single point — their first winless, pointless World Cup group stage, a stark reversal from the team Bora Milutinović had guided past Scotland and Sweden in 1990.

2006.homeColors: red shirt with white and blue brushstroke trim, blue shorts, white socks (Joma)
2006.awayColors: white shirt with blue vertical stripes, white shorts (Joma)

2014.headline: Los Ticos Stun the World

annotations.label: GROUP OF DEATH SURVIVORS
annotations.story: Drawn with Uruguay, Italy and England — three former World Cup winners inside the top 10 of the FIFA rankings — Costa Rica were tipped to finish last. Instead Bryan Ruiz's headed winner over Italy and Joel Campbell's strike past Uruguay made Los Ticos only the second team, after Denmark, to beat two champions in one group stage.

annotations.label: THE GOALKEEPER SWAP
annotations.story: With the quarter-final locked at 0-0 after extra time, Dutch manager Louis van Gaal used his final substitution to bring on Tim Krul purely for the penalty shootout. Krul saved from Bryan Ruiz and Michael Umaña, and the gamble sent the Netherlands through 4-3 on kicks.

annotations.label: A JERSEY FOR THE RUN
annotations.story: Lotto's 2014 kit paired traditional home red with blue-and-white accents, and after Los Ticos' shock run to the quarter-finals, the Italian manufacturer issued a special commemorative jersey to mark the achievement — a rare honor for a team seeded to finish last in its group.

2014.homeColors: red shirt with blue front stripe, blue shorts, red socks (Lotto)
2014.awayColors: white shirt with red and blue trim, white shorts (Lotto)

2018.headline: Waston Ends a Long Drought

annotations.label: BAR, HEAD, GOAL
annotations.story: Bryan Ruiz's stoppage-time penalty against Switzerland cannoned back off the crossbar and struck goalkeeper Yann Sommer's head on the way in, credited as an own goal. It salvaged a 2-2 draw and made Sommer only the third goalkeeper in World Cup history to score an own goal.

annotations.label: NEW BALANCE'S FIRST CUP
annotations.story: 2018 marked New Balance's first World Cup as Costa Rica's kit supplier, dressing a squad built around the same Bryan Ruiz and Keylor Navas generation that had stunned the world in Brazil four years earlier, this time exiting without a win.

annotations.label: A CHAMPIONS LEAGUE KEEPER
annotations.story: Keylor Navas started 2018 fresh off winning a third consecutive Champions League with Real Madrid — the first Costa Rican to hold Europe's biggest club prize — only to concede seven goals in three World Cup games as the team's defense collapsed after their 2014 heroics.

2018.homeColors: red shirt, blue shorts, white socks — 'Declare Your DNA' design (New Balance)
2018.awayColors: white shirt with navy sleeves, evoking the black-and-white 1990 alternate kit (New Balance)

2022.headline: Fuller's Flash, Then a Rout

annotations.label: SEVEN WITHOUT REPLY
annotations.story: Spain scored with each of their first seven shots on target, romping to a 7-0 win that equaled Costa Rica's worst defeat in team history, a 1975 loss to Mexico. Costa Rica did not manage a single shot of their own across the full ninety minutes.

annotations.label: NEW BALANCE'S LAST DANCE
annotations.story: Qatar 2022 was New Balance's final World Cup as Costa Rica's kit maker before Adidas took over in 2023 — a quiet sign-off after a tournament that opened with the joint-heaviest defeat in the team's history.

annotations.label: FULLER'S FLASH
annotations.story: Four days after conceding seven to Spain, Costa Rica beat Japan 1–0 on a second-half strike from Keysher Fuller — a result that briefly kept their knockout hopes alive and remains one of the most improbable turnarounds of the group stage.

2022.homeColors: red shirt with blue sleeve cuffs, blue shorts (New Balance)
2022.awayColors: white shirt with blue sleeve cuffs, white shorts with blue stripe (New Balance)
